title-group.article-title: A Murine Model of Cardiovascular-Kidney-Metabolic Syndrome Demonstrates Compromised Limb Function in the Ischemic Hind Limb

contrib-group.aff: 1Renal Section, Department of Medicine, Boston University School of Medicine, Boston, Massachusetts

6Division of Vascular Surgery, Department of Surgery, Cardiovascular Institute, Stanford University School of Medicine, Stanford, California
7Department of Biostatistics, School of Public Health, Boston, Massachusetts

author-notes.corresp: Correspondence: Dr. Vipul Chitalia, email: vichital@bu.edu

related-article.article-title: A Novel Murine Model Integrating Peripheral Arterial Disease and Multiorgan Dysfunction

list-item.p: Cardiovascular-kidney-metabolic (CKM) is a global public health problem; however, its mediators remain poorly known, in part due to the lack of a reliable animal model.
A combination of high fat and adenine diet recapitulate some of the CKD and metabolic phenotypes of CKM.
This model also demonstrates myocardial fibrosis and peripheral artery disease with sex-based differences and can be leveraged to probe mechanisms of CKM.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Cardiovascular-kidney-metabolic (CKM) syndrome is a public health problem in the United States and results in premature cardiovascular disease at a relatively preserved GFR. The molecular mediators of CKM are poorly understood, partly due to the lack of a reliable animal model. We set out to generate an animal model with renal and metabolic dysfunctions, using peripheral artery disease (PAD) as a CKM manifestation.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: C57BL/6 male and female mice were randomized into four groups: a normal diet (controls), a 0.2% adenine diet (AD, a CKD model), a high-fat diet (HFD, a metabolic model), and a combination of HFD+AD (a potential CKM model). The mice underwent a hind limb ischemia, followed by an array of structural, endurance, and postexercise hyperemia assays.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Compared with control mice, HFD+AD male mice had 23%–50% higher weight and GFR than the AD group (P = 0.003). The kidneys of HFD+AD showed tubular atrophy, tubulointerstitial fibrosis, immune infiltration, glomerulomegaly, consistent with glomerular hyperperfusion, hypercholesterolemia, impaired glucose tolerance, and adipophilin in the liver, an early marker of hepatic steatosis, and myocardial fibrosis. The HFD+AD mice showed reductions in the hind limb perfusion ratios, microcapillary density, type 2 muscle fibers, and increased muscle fibrosis, immune infiltration, and lowest cross-sectional muscle area. Female CKM mice revealed distinct differences from male mice. Compared with AD and HFD alone, female CKM mice exposed to HFD+AD demonstrated additive phenotypes in endurance assays (distance traveled, exhaustion time, and grip strength) without a similar effect in postischemia perfusion, suggesting skeletal muscle, and microcapillary dysfunction.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: A combination of HFD+AD in mice displays features of CKD, metabolic disorders, and cardiovascular disease at a higher GFR, consistent with human CKM. This model can be explored to probe the mechanisms and heterogeneity and sex-specific differences in CKM.

award-group.funding-source: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases
National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases
National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases
National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases
Multidisciplinary Training Program in Cardiovascular Epidemiology
National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases
National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases
National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases
National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases
Multidisciplinary Training Program in Cardiovascular Epidemiology

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: The interactions between CKD, metabolic disorders (obesity and diabetes), and cardiovascular disease are well established. Recently, these morbidities are considered holistically as cardiovascular-kidney-metabolic (CKM) syndrome. CKM is defined as a health disorder due to connections among heart disease, CKD, diabetes, and obesity, leading to earlier presentation of cardiovascular disease (CVD) and poor health outcomes at a relatively preserved renal function.1,2

The CKM is recognized due to an increasing burden of obesity, diabetes, and CKD in the United States.3 Studies have shown that severe obesity with a body mass index of 40–45 reduces survival by 8–10 years, diabetes by 13–40 years, and stages 4 and 5 CKD by more than 20 years.4 These patients suffer from a combination of these comorbidities (multimorbidities). A nationwide sample showed 10-year mortality rates for diabetes alone was 7.7%, CKD was 11.5%, and a combination of diabetes and CKD was approximately double the sum of its parts (31.1%).4 There is a change in the trend of CVD in CKM in the population.3,5 The National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey from 1988 to 2018 identified ten cardiometabolic and renal phenotypes. The prevalence of the “high BP” and “high cholesterol” phenotypes decreased over time, contrasted by a rise in the “obesity” and “low eGFR” phenotypes. One of the manifestations of CKM is peripheral artery disease (PAD).6 PAD affects over 230 million worldwide and is linked to an increased risk of adverse outcomes, including other CVDs, and adverse limb events such as gangrene and amputation.7 We have shown that CKD significantly affects postischemic angiogenesis in a rodent model of hind limb ischemia (HLI).8

Despite its profound public health implications, the mediators of CKM, especially for prevalent phenotypes of low GFR and dysfunctional obesity, remain poorly defined. Although there are rodent models combining CKD and metabolic features, none of these models characterized the PAD as a manifestation of CKM.9,10 Some of these CKD models do not demonstrate obesity or occasionally have an opposite phenotype, such as weight loss (in an adenine-induced CKD model). We set out to generate an animal model of CKM using PAD as a manifestation. In this model, we combined diet-induced obesity and CKD with a hypothesis that such a model will emulate CKM syndrome with metabolic abnormalities and CVD at an earlier stage of CKD (a hallmark of CKM syndrome in humans).

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: All the experiments were performed with Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee IPROTO202200000072 at Boston Medical Center and Boston University School of Medicine. We followed the National Institutes of Health (NIH) Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals. The details for Image J processing, exercise test, grip test, and Delta Flux and Doppler Laser studies are described in the Supplemental Methods.

sec.title: HLI
sec.p: A detailed procedure for HLI, a well-established model of PAD has been previously described.8

sec.title: Animal Models
sec.p: A group of 8- to 12-week-old C57BL/6 mice (Jackson's Lab, catalog 000664) was randomized into four groups. They were fed a casein diet for a week, followed by casein-based purified diets (see Supplemental Table 1 for the composition of the diet, Research Diets, NJ) for six consecutive weeks. CKD was induced with 0.2% adenine diet (AD), characterized by profound tubulointerstitial fibrosis and retention of uremic solutes like the patients with ESKD.8,11 Adenine is converted to 2,8-dihydroxy adenine in the liver, which crystalizes in the renal interstitium due to the high salt content of the rodent urine. This process results in profound tubular atrophy and interstitial fibrosis.12–15 This model mimics crystal-induced tubular damage and interstitial fibrosis in CKD patients with atheroembolic disease or cholesterol embolisms.16,17 In addition, tubular atrophy and interstitial fibrosis are the final common pathway for CKD, irrespective of inciting etiology. The adenine-induced CKD model represents advanced CKD.16,17

Obesity was induced using a high-fat diet (HFD). Mice on a casein-based diet (normal diet [ND]) served as a control. A combination of HFD+AD was investigated as CKM syndrome. Diets were initiated for 1 week before HLI and were continued for 4 weeks.

sec.title: Immunohistochemistry and Immunofluorescence and Quantification of Features
sec.p: For immunohistochemistry and immunofluorescence, samples were processed as previously described.11,18 Antibodies are listed in Supplemental Table 2.

sec.title: GFR Measurement Using FITC-Sinistrin
sec.p: BUN and creatinine are established measures of kidney function in mice and humans. However, these measures are dependent on several factors, including protein intake and skeletal muscle mass, and are less sensitive. The adenine-induced CKD model is characterized by the loss of appetite and sarcopenia, which can confound the BUN and creatinine tests. We used a 10 mg/100 g of FITC-Sinistrin (Medibeacon FITC-Sinistrin) for GFR measurement through retro-orbital bolus.19 The following formula was used.20–22 The GFR was normalized to body weight.GFR [μL/⁡min⁡/100g b.w.]=14616.8 [μL/100g b.w.]t1/2 (FITC‐sinistrin) [⁡min⁡]

sec.title: Glucose Intolerance Test
sec.p: An oral glucose was injected per the protocol described previously.23–25

sec.title: Statistical Analysis
sec.p: Statistical analysis was conducted using SAS version 9.3 (SAS Institute). Data are presented as mean±SEM, or as median with range and 25th and 75th percentiles in violin plots. Group comparisons were assessed using an ANOVA or two-tailed Student t test. Multiple comparison adjustment was performed using Bonferroni correction or Tukey-Kramer test. The linear mixed models were performed for analysis focused on the time-by-group interaction, comparing and testing for differences in the mean patterns across groups. Finally, a post hoc analysis was performed for the P values for pairwise comparisons between groups at the final time point and adjusted for multiple hypothesis testing at that time point. Statistical significance was defined as P ≤ 0.05 or 0.025 or 0.0167, depending on multiple comparisons.

sec.title: Characterization of Kidneys in Different Groups of Mice
sec.p: The mice's body weights were recorded weekly (Figure 1A). A mixed linear model showed a significant interaction between time and group. The prerandomization weights were similar in all groups. The mice on ND (control) showed a minimal increase in weight over the experiment. However, mice on HFD showed a persistent increase in body weight, which was higher by approximately 20% compared with the control group at the end of the experiment (ND mice=26.23±1.7 g, HFD 30.493+2.374 g, P = 0.03). Mice on AD showed a consistent reduction in weight from approximately 23 to 15.4±1.2 g over the 6 weeks (P < 0.0001 compared with control). Mice on HFD+AD had an increase in body weight in the first 2 weeks, followed by a reduction at the end of the experiment, with the final body weight lower than that of AD (HFD+AD=22.46±2.49 g, P < 0.001) and HFD (P = 0.003).

caption.p: Characterization of kidneys of mice from four different groups. (A) The average weights of mice in all four groups are shown. Error bars are SEM. The mixed linear model was followed by a post hoc analysis for different group comparisons, which were adjusted for multiple comparisons (P < 0.0001). (B) Representative graphs of GFR normalized to the body weight of mice measured using FITC-Sinistrin dye per group are shown. (C) The average GFR normalized to the body weight of mice measured using FITC-Sinistrin dye is shown. The mixed linear model showed time and group interaction. Tukey-Kramer adjustment was performed for multiple comparisons. (D) Kidneys were stained with hematoxylin and eosin from all four groups. Scale bar=100 μm. (E) Representative images of kidneys stained with modified Masson Trichome are shown. Scale bar=100 μm. (F) The glomerular surface area measurements on three hpf images from each kidney of mice were performed ANOVA, P < 0.0001. Post hoc Student t test was performed to compare the groups. Bonferroni correction was applied. ND and HFD+AD P < 0.0001, AD versus AD+HFD ****P < 0.0001, ND versus HFD (***P = 0.0096). (G) IFTA scores measured from three hpf images across four experimental groups of mice are shown. ANOVA analysis showed P < 0.0001. Post hoc Student t test was performed to compare the groups. Bonferroni correction was applied. ND versus AD P < 0.0001, ND versus HFD+AD P < 0.0001. (H) Tubular area measurements across all experimental groups are shown. ANOVA P < 0.0001. Post hoc Student t test was performed to compare the groups. Bonferroni correction applied. The kidneys tubular surface area in AD versus control (P = 0.003) and HFD+AD groups compared with ND (P = 0.005). (I) The normalized integrated density of inflammation (per μm2) was obtained from three hpf per kidney per mouse in each group. ANOVA P = 0.2161. Post hoc Student t test was performed to compare the groups. Bonferroni correction was applied. AD compared with ND (P = 0.005) and HFD+AD compared with ND (P = 0.0019). ***P < 0.01 and ****P < 0.0001. AD, adenine diet; H&E, hematoxylin and eosin; HFD, high-fat diet; hpf, high power field; IFTA, interstitial fibrosis and tubular atrophy; ND, normal diet.

sec.p: Given the changes in the lean body weights in different groups, we measured GFR normalized to body weight weekly using FITC-Sinistrin dye (Figure 1B). Faster dye clearance suggests a higher GFR level, while delayed dye clearance points to a reduced GFR. Control mice had GFR unchanged over 42 days, while HFD mice showed persistently higher GFR (GFR control=345.51±52.53 μl/min, HFD 363.75±57.51, P = 0.03, Figure 1C). As expected, mice on AD showed a drastic reduction in GFR in the first 14 days, and it plateaued to be around 10% GFR at the beginning of the study (29.41±4.4 μl/min). Mice with HFD+AD followed a similar trend to AD mice. However, at each time point, the GFR values were greater in HFD+AD mice compared with mice on AD (preharvest 58.53±5.10 μl/min, P < 0.0001, Figure 1C). Final BUN in the control group was 23.10±4.45 mg/dl; in the AD group, it was 134.2±7.24 mg/dl; in the HFD group, it was 28.80±2.77 mg/dl; in the HFD +AD group, and it was 96.60±0.89 mg/dl. Creatinine in ND was 0.10±0.00 mg/dl, in AD was 1.26±0.23 mg/dl, in HFD was 0.30±0.27 mg/dl, and in HFD+AD was 0.72±0.08 mg/dl.

The kidneys of mice on AD showed global glomerulosclerosis, tubule-interstitial fibrosis, loss of brush border, dilated tubules, tubular atrophy, and immune cell infiltration. Mice on HFD showed glomerulomegaly and minimal immune infiltration. The mice exposed to HFD+AD had features of CKD along with glomerulomegaly (Figure 1, D and E). The kidneys of mice on AD and HFD+AD showed more interstitial fibrosis (blue color on the trichrome stain) compared with other groups. The morphologic features of kidneys were quantitated by a nephropathologist blinded to the group of mice (M. Belghasem). Compared with the control mice, the glomerular surface area was higher in HFD and HFD+AD (Figure 1F). The interstitial fibrosis and tubular atrophy (IFTA) were quantitated by the intensity and distribution of fibrosis along with tubular features, as done previously.26 A scale of 1–5 was used, where a score of 5 indicated IFTA affecting 100% of the renal parenchyma. Compared with the control, mice on AD and HFD+AD showed significantly higher IFTA (Figure 1G). The kidneys showed tubular dilatation due to tubular cell atrophy, which was higher in AD and HFD+AD groups compared with the control (Figure 1H). The immune infiltration was significantly higher in AD and HFD+AD groups compared with the control (Figure 1I). The HFD+AD mice demonstrate changes consistent with a combination of glomerulomegaly, a pathognomic feature of obesity, and renal damage (IFTA and reduced GFR).

sec.title: Characterization of Metabolic Derangements and Cardiac Alterations in Different Groups of Mice
sec.p: CKM is characterized by dyslipidemia, glucose intolerance, and evidence of hepatic steatosis. At the end of the experiment, compared with the ND group, serum cholesterol showed a trend toward higher levels in AD; compared with control, HFD and HFD+AD showed a significant increase in serum cholesterol levels. A 2.5-fold increase in cholesterol was observed in the HFD+AD group compared with AD (P < 0.0001). By contrast, triglyceride levels remained similar between groups (Figure 2, A and B). The control and AD groups showed a similar glucose tolerance, while HFD and HFD+AD showed an impaired glucose tolerance (Figure 2C). Adipophilin binds to cytoplasmic lipid droplets and is an early marker of hepatic steatosis.27 Compared with mice on ND, the livers of mice on HFD and HFD+AD showed—doubling of adipophilin expression (Figure 2, D and E). Myocardium was stained for collagen deposition, a pathologic feature associated with heart pathologies, including heart fibrosis (Figure 2F).28 The hearts of AD and HFD+AD mice showed a significant increase in collagen deposition (on Sirius red stain; Figure 2G) compared with the control group. Taken together, mice on HFD+AD develop hypercholesterolemia, impaired glucose tolerance, and liver steatosis, demonstrating the metabolic derangements consistent with CKM syndrome, while the AD-induced CKD model lacked some of these features. In addition, CKM mice showed early evidence of myocardial fibrosis.

caption.p: Characterization of metabolic parameters and heart morphology in four groups. (A) Average cholesterol levels are shown. Error bars—SEM. One-way ANOVA P < 0.001. Post hoc Student t test was performed to compare the groups. Bonferroni correction was applied. ND versus HFD **P = 0.003, ND versus HFD+AD **P = 0.002, and HFD versus HFD+AD P = 0.0436. (B) Average triglyceride levels are shown. Error bars—SEM. (C) Glucose tolerance test with average and SEM of glucose at indicated time points after glucose load are shown. ANOVA P < 0.001. At 15 minutes, blood glucose levels were significantly higher in HFD and HFD+AD groups compared with ND P < 0.0001. At 30 and 60 minutes, ND and both HFD and AD groups P < 0.001 for both time points. At 120 minutes, ND and HFD P = 0.039. No significant differences were observed between other group comparisons across all time points. (D) Representative liver sections stained for adipophilin from four groups of mice are shown. Scale bar=100 μm. (E) Adipophilin expression was quantified in liver biopsies of all four groups as integrated density normalized to area. Average and SEM are shown. ANOVA <0.0001. Post hoc Student's t test was performed to compare the groups. Bonferroni correction was applied. ND versus AD ****P < 0.0001, ND versus AD+HFD ***P = 0.0026. ND versus HFD ***P = 0.0028. (F) Representative myocardial sections stained with trichrome and Sirius red from four groups of mice are shown. Scale bar=100 μm. Trichrome-stained images display fibrosis in the heart valve and subendocardial region. (G) The averages of myocardial collagen quantified from three high-power fields of Sirius red-stained myocardium per mouse are shown as integrated density normalized to the surface area. ANOVA P value of 0.0256. Post hoc Student t test was performed to compare the groups. Bonferroni correction was applied. Compared with ND, the AD group *P = 0.0193 and the HFD+AD group (*P = 0.0219).

sec.title: PAD Manifestation in Different Groups of Mice
sec.p: In the HLI model, perfusion recovery serves as a biologic readout of postischemic angiogenesis. It is presented as a ratio of perfusion in the ligated to the unligated limb, indicating postischemic angiogenesis. For this experiment, mice were initiated on different diets for 1 week before HLI, and then, the diets were continued through the study period. The preprocedural perfusion ratios were approximately 1 and reduced to 10% of the preprocedure values in all groups in the immediate postprocedural records. In the control group, the perfusion increased by 50% and 65% of the preprocedural value at the end of the experiment (Figure 3A and Supplemental Figure 1A). The perfusion ratio was higher at day 14 after HLI in HFD and then plateaued. Compared with control, the perfusion ratios of mice in HFD+AD were approximately 15% higher at the baseline and then reduced at day 28. At the end of 28 days, compared with control, both AD and HFD+AD showed approximately 50% reduction in the perfusion ratio. There was no significant difference in the perfusion ratio between the AD and the HFD+AD groups (Figure 3A and Supplemental Figure 1A).

caption.p: CKM mice demonstrate reduced perfusion ratio and microcapillary density the ischemic hind limbs. (A) The average and SEM of the perfusion ratio from all experimental groups are shown. A linear effects model showed a significant interaction between perfusion ratio and time P < 0.0001. Post hoc analysis with Tukey-Kramer adjustment indicated that at day 28, the ND versus AD P < 0.0001, ND versus HFD+AD group P = 0.0315. The AD versus the HFD+AD group P = 0.0036. (B) Three random hpfs images from soleus muscles of the ischemic limb of each mouse (n=7 mice/group) stained with CD31 and MYH4 are shown. White arrowheads are directed at CD31+ capillaries. Scale bars=100 μm. (C) The normalized CD31 to MYH4 expression is shown. Post hoc Student t test was performed to compare the groups. Bonferroni correction was applied. ND versus AD (P < 0.0001), versus HFD (P < 0.0001) and versus the HFD+AD groups (P < 0.0001). (D) Three random hpf images from soleus muscles of the ischemic limb of each mouse (n=7 mice/group) stained with CD45 and α actin are shown. White asterisks correspond to CD45 expression. Scale bars=100 μm. (E) The average CD45 expression normalized to α actin was quantified as integrated density. One-way ANOVA P < 0.0001. Post hoc Student's t test was performed to compare the groups. Bonferroni correction was applied. Compared with the AD group, HFD+AD P = 0.0002. ND versus AD P = 0.0220, ND versus HFD P = 0.002. **P < 0.05, ***P < 0.01, and ****P < 0.0001. CKM, cardiovascular-kidney-metabolic.

sec.p: The perfusion to the lower extremity is based on the microcapillary development in the skeletal muscles of the ischemic hind limb. The capillary density was examined in the soleus muscle of ischemic and nonischemic limbs in all four groups using CD31, a marker of endothelial cells, and normalized to MYH4 (a marker of skeletal muscle). MYH4 and CD31 expressions were quantitated as the integrated density (Figure 3, B and C).8,11 Compared with the control, the normalized CD31 expression was reduced to 50% in AD, 15%–23% in HFD, and 45% in the HFD+AD groups. To probe the muscle immune microenvironment, the soleus was stained using a CD45 antibody, a marker of leukocytes. The ND mice showed minimal expression of CD45, while other groups had a 30%–80% higher expression of CD45, with the highest in CKM mice (Figure 3, D and E). The HFD+AD group showed a higher CD45 expression. These results suggested that CKM mice showed compromised angiogenesis similar to AD mice at a GFR value—double that of the AD group (Figure 1C) and the highest immune cell infiltration (an additive effect of HFD+AD compared with HFD alone or AD alone).

sec.title: Characterization of Myopathy in Different Groups of Mice
sec.p: Skeletal muscle damage in these mice can be an effect of compromised angiogenesis and a direct effect of CKD and metabolic factors. The cross-sectional areas (CSAs) of the soleus muscles of nonischemic and ischemic limbs were compared in four groups. On the nonischemic limb, AD and HFD+AD showed a 10%–15% reduction in CSA compared with the control mice (Figure 4, A and B). The mean CSA of the soleus of control mice was 1200 µm2, and AD, HFD, and HFD+AD were 900, 1100, and 800 µm2, respectively. This phenotype was exacerbated by ischemia (Figure 4, C and D). Compared with the nonischemic group, the ischemic limb soleus of all groups showed lower CSA (Figure 4, B and D). Compared with the ischemic soleus from control mice, AD and HFD mice had approximately 50% reduction in CSA with HFD+AD having the lowest CSA (500 µm2), suggesting severe muscle atrophy in this group.

caption.p: Characterization of myocytes in four groups of mice. (A) Representative images of soleus muscle on the sham-operated limbs (non-ischemic) stained with H&E and obtained at two different magnifications are shown. (B) The average CSA of nonischemic soleus muscle obtained from each mouse's five random hpf images is shown. Error bars—SEM. One-way ANOVA P = 0.0001. Post hoc Student t test was performed to compare the groups. Bonferroni correction was applied. Compared with ND, the AD group P = 0.0067 and the HFD+AD group P = 0.0001. (C) Representative images of soleus muscle on the HLI-operated limbs (ischemic) stained as above. Scale bar=100 μm. (D) The average CSA of the ischemic soleus muscle obtained from five random hpf images of each mouse is shown. Error bars—SEM. One-way ANOVA P = 0.0001. Post hoc Student t test was performed to compare the groups. Bonferroni correction was applied. ND versus AD (P = 0.003), and versus HFD+AD (P < 0.0001). (E) Percentage of muscle fibers with central nuclei across experimental groups in the ischemic and non-ischemic muscle tissue. Three random hpf images per mouse were examined in a manner blinded to the group. Two-way ANOVA showed P < 0.0001. Post hoc Student t test was performed to compare the groups. Bonferroni correction was applied. In nonischemic muscles, no significant differences were observed. Compared with ND, the AD group P = 0.0032, and HFD+AD P = 0.0395 in the ischemic muscle. **P < 0.05, ***P < 0.01, ****P < 0.0001. CSA, cross-sectional area; HLI, hind limb ischemia.

sec.p: The ischemic insult is associated with the proliferation of skeletal muscles characterized by the central nucleus. As expected, the soleus on nonischemic limbs showed almost negligible proliferating fibers (Figure 4E), which increased with the injury. In the ischemic group, the percentage of muscle fibers with central nuclei in control mice was approximately 20% of total fibers, which was reduced by more than 80% in AD and by 50% in HFD+AD diets, suggesting compromised regeneration of skeletal muscles in these groups. The percentage of cells with central nuclei was calculated in 100 fibers in each mouse.

Muscle atrophy is associated with fibrotic tissue. We further examined muscle fibrosis in nonischemic and ischemic limbs using a modified Masson Trichrome stain (Figure 5, A and B). In the nonischemic group, the soleus of AD and HFD+AD showed an increase in fibrosis compared with controls (Figure 5C). In the control group, the ischemic soleus showed a 30% increase in fibrosis compared with the nonischemic group. In the ischemic limb, compared with controls, mice in AD and HFD+AD groups showed a 60% to a four-fold increase in fibrosis (Figure 5C). Soleus predominantly contains glycolytic type 2 fibers, which were identified using MYH7.29 In the ischemic limb, compared with the control group, type 2 fibers were significantly reduced by 50% and 80% in AD and HFD+AD groups, respectively (Figure 5, D and E). The HFD+AD group showed 30% lower MYH7 expression compared with the AD group. These results demonstrate the compromised regenerative ability of the ischemic muscles and muscle fibrosis in all groups of mice and the highest muscle atrophy and immune infiltration in the HFD+AD group.

caption.p: Muscle fibrosis in four groups of mice. (A) Representative images from three random hpf images of trichrome-stained sections from non-ischemic muscle tissues are shown. (n=7 mice/group), scale bars=100 μm. (B) Representative images of trichrome-stained sections from ischemic soleus muscle tissues as above are shown. (C) The muscle fibrosis was quantified in the trichrome-stained muscles, and three random trichrome-stained hpf images were used to quantify fibrosis. Fibrosis is represented as the integrated density of fibrosis normalized to the surface area. One-way ANOVA (P < 0.0001). Post hoc Student t test was performed to compare the groups. Bonferroni correction was applied. In the nonischemic group, compared with ND, the AD (P < 0.0001), and HFD+AD P < 0.0001. In the ischemic group, one-way ANOVA (P < 0.0001). Compared with ND, the AD (P < 0.0001), and HFD+AD (P < 0.0001). Between the ischemic and non-ischemic ND groups P = 0.012. In the ischemic group, compared with ND, HFD+AD P = 0.0037, AD P = 0.0039. (D) Representative images of ischemic soleus muscle stained from three random images (n=7 mice/group) from the ligated limbs are shown. Scale bars=100 μm. (E) The MYH7 expression is represented as integrated density normalized to the surface area. One-way ANOVA P value of <0.0001. Post hoc Student t test was performed to compare the groups. Bonferroni correction was applied. Compared with the ND group, the AD group P = 0.0046) and the HFD+AD group P < 0.0001. **P < 0.05, ***P < 0.01, and ****P < 0.0001.

sec.title: Functional Characterization of PAD in All Groups of Mice
sec.p: Exercise is associated with skeletal muscle hyperemia, which is compromised in patients with PAD, resulting in vascular claudication.30 This phenomenon is mimicked in mice as the reduction in post compared with pre-exercise perfusion (delta flux ratio).31 The delta flux values were examined in all groups of mice at the end of experiment day 28 (Figure 6A and Supplemental Figure 1B). Compared with the control group, the AD, HFD, and HFD+AD groups showed consistently lower delta flux, with the lowest delta flux noted in the HFD+AD group (Figure 6A).

caption.p: Functional characterization of the PAD model. (A) The average delta flux calculated as a difference between postexercise and pre-exercise perfusion are shown. Error bars=SEM. The mixed linear model analysis P < 0.0001. Tukey-Kramer adjustment for multiple comparisons: At day 28, compared with the ND group, AD P = 0.0001, HFD=0.0017, and HFD+AD P < 0.0001. (B) Distance traveled in meters at different time points are shown as average and error bars=SEM. A linear mixed model showed a significant interaction between time and groups P = 0.0001. The Tukey-Kramer adjustment was performed for multiple comparisons. At day 21, compared with the ND group, AD group P = 0.038, HFD P = 0.0449, and HFD+AD=0.002. At 28 days, compared with ND, AD=0.0001, HFD and HFD+AD <0.0001. (C) The average time to exhaustion among different groups of mice is shown. A mixed linear effect model demonstrated a significant interaction between time and group (P < 0.0001). At day 28, time to exhaustion was significantly reduced in all groups compared with ND, with P values as follows: AD=0.0001, HFD+AD <0.0001, and AD <0.0001. (D) The average grip strength of the mice's hind limb is depicted as grams/force at indicated time points. Error bars=SEM. The mixed linear model showed a significant interaction between the time and different groups (pre-HLI P = 0.01, preharvest P = 0.03). Pre-HLI time point, compared with ND, the AD, and the HFD+AD groups (P = 0.023 and P = 0.031, respectively). At the end of the experiment, compared with the ND and AD groups, the HFD+AD (P = 0.018 and P = 0.036, respectively). *P < 0.05, ***P < 0.01, and ****P < 0.0001.

sec.p: The distance traveled (meters) and time to exhaustion (minutes) in all groups were recorded. Both these parameters showed reduction within 7 days of HLI procedure, after which they improved in mice on control (Figure 6, B and C). In the control mice, the traveled distance increased from approximately 180 m on day 7 to approximately 220 m on day 14, approximately 300 m on day 21, and approximately 400 m on day 28. However, for the mice on HFD and HFD+AD, the travel distances increased over time but were lower than those of the control group of mice. The CKD mice showed consistently the lowest traveled distance. At the end of the experiment, all groups showed lower traveled distance compared with control, and there was no significant difference between AD and HFD+AD groups.

Time to exhaustion followed a pattern similar to travel distance. The exhaustion time in control mice was approximately 12 minutes on day 7, approximately 14 minutes on day 14, approximately 18 minutes on day 21, and approximately 25 minutes on day 28. By contrast, the exhaustion time remained consistently lower in other groups of mice. At the end of the experiment, there was no significant difference in the exhaustion time between AD and HFD+AD diet mice (Figure 6C). For the grip strength assessment, we measured the force generated in grams (g) at three time points (Figure 6D). The baseline grip (prerandomization) strength was similar in all groups. The mixed linear model revealed a significant interaction between time and the different groups (pre-HLI, P = 0.01; pre-harvest, P = 0.03). At the pre-HLI time point, compared with the control group, the AD, and the HFD+AD groups, showed a 20%–30% reduction. At the end of the experiment, the HFD+AD group showed approximately 15%–30% reduced grip strength compared with the control and AD groups, suggesting severe limb dysfunction in HFD+AD.

sec.title: Sex-Based Differences in the CKM Model
sec.p: To investigate the sex differences in CKM phenotypes related to PAD, we performed similar experiments in female C57BL/6 mice divided in four groups. The kidneys of female mice showed pathologic features such as male mice (Supplemental Figure 2A). The glomerular surface area was significantly higher in HFD (approximately 50% increase) and HFD+AD compared with controls (Supplemental Figure 2B). A significantly higher IFTA scores were noted in AD mice (Supplemental Figure 2C). Tubular dilatation was significantly increased in AD and HFD+AD compared with control (Supplemental Figure 2D). Immune cell infiltration did not show significant differences between groups (Supplemental Figure 2E). The observed morphologic alterations in the HFD+AD group suggest a combination of obesity-related glomerulomegaly and progressive renal damage. In contrast to the male mice, no myocardial fibrosis was observed in the female mice (Supplemental Figure 1F). Like male mice, female mice demonstrated an increase in adipophilin in the HFD and HFD+AD groups and a decrease in the adipophilin in the AD group (Figure 2F and Supplemental Figure 2G).

We further examined the skeletal muscles in female mice. Like male mice, female mice in AD and HFD+AD groups showed higher fibrosis compared with the control diet group (approximately 30 times) and the HFD group (approximately six times; Supplemental Figure 3A). Skeletal muscle regeneration was examined using the percentage of fibers with central nuclei (Supplemental Figure 3B). Unlike male mice, no reduction in the percentage of fibers with central nuclei in AD, HFD, or HFD+AD group compared with the control group was observed, suggesting better skeletal muscle regenerative capacity in female mice after the HLI model. There was a significant reduction in normalized capillary density in AD and HFD+AD mice compared with control mice (Figure 7A and Supplemental Figure 3C). No additive effect was observed in HFD+AD compared with HFD or AD-alone groups. Taken together, compared with male mice, female mice in the HFD+AD group showed no myocardial fibrosis or suppression of proliferative skeletal muscle fibers.

caption.p: Functional characterization of the PAD model in female mice. (A) The normalized CD31 to MYH4 expression in ND versus AD (P < 0.0001) and versus the HFD+AD groups (P = 0.0013). (B) The average and SEM of the perfusion ratio from all female experimental groups are shown. A linear effects model showed a significant interaction between perfusion ratio and time P < 0.0001. Post hoc analysis with Tukey-Kramer adjustment indicated that at day 28, the HFD versus HFD+AD group (P = 0.0153). (C) The delta flux calculated as a difference between postexercise and pre-exercise perfusion are shown. Error bars=SEM. Tukey-Kramer adjustment for multiple comparisons was performed: at day 28, ANOVA P value = 0.0047. (D) Average distance traveled in meters at different time points are shown. Error bars—SEM. One-way ANOVA P value = 0.0004. Post hoc Student t test was performed to compare the groups. Bonferroni correction was applied. HFD versus HFD+AD P = 0.0001, control versus HFD+AD P = 0.0005, and AD versus HFD+AD P = 0.0116. (E) Average time to exhaustion in minutes at different time points are shown. Error bars—SEM. One-way ANOVA P = 0.0006. Post hoc Student t test was performed to compare the groups. Bonferroni correction was applied. HFD+AD versus HFD P = 0.0138, HFD+AD versus AD P = 0.0080, and HFD+AD versus ND P < 0.0001. (F) Average gram-force in grams at different time points are shown. Error bars—SEM. One-way ANOVA P < 0.0001. Post hoc Student t test was performed to compare the groups. Bonferroni correction was applied. HFD+AD versus HFD P < 0.0001, HFD+AD versus AD P = 0.0104, and HFD+AD versus ND, P = 0.0006. *P <0.05, ***P < 0.001, and ****P < 0.0001.

sec.p: Functional characterization of the PAD model was performed using different assays. Unlike male mice, the female mice in the control group showed almost complete restoration of perfusion by the end of the experiment (Figure 7B and Supplemental Figure 3D). A significant reduction in the perfusion ratio was observed in AD group compared with the control group (P = 0.0016), and HFD+AD compared with HFD alone (P = 0.013). There was 30%–35% reduction in perfusion ratio compared in the HFD+AD diet compared with HFD alone group. The postexercise hyperemia (delta flux) was examined at the end of the experiment after HLI (Figure 7C and Supplemental Figure 3E). Mice on ND showed maximal increase with exercise. Although other groups showed nonsignificant lower delta flux compared with controls, the highest reduction (reversal) in delta flux was observed in the HFD+AD diet compared with the control group.

The distance traveled (meters) and time to exhaustion (minutes) in all groups were recorded. Both these parameters were highest in control mice. The HFD+AD female mice consistently showed the significantly lowest traveled distance and shorter exhaustion time compared with control, AD, and HFD alone groups (Figure 7, D and E). The HFD+AD group showed approximately 35% reduction in the grip strength versus the AD group, approximately 55% versus HFD and control groups (Figure 7F). The above assays suggest a lack of additive effect of the HFD+AD diet in female mice in the perfusion ratio, while the distance traveled, time to exhaustion, and force generated showed a consistent additive effect of a combination of HFD and AD diet.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Our study demonstrates that a combination of AD and HFD in mice induces some of the key features of CKM syndrome. This mouse model resulted in histopathologic and functional changes in kidneys (IFTA, immune infiltration, reduced GFR), and metabolic dysfunction (hypercholesterolemia, glucose intolerance, nonalcoholic hepatic steatosis, and glomerulomegaly). This model also manifested with myocardial fibrosis and PAD (impaired angiogenesis, reduced endurance, and postexercise reactive hyperemia). The HFD+AD mice exhibited peripheral vascular and hind limb dysfunction at a relatively preserved GFR, mimicking CKM patients who develop premature cardiovascular disease with a relatively higher GFR.1–3,5,6 Although the HFD+AD mice did not develop overt obesity, they exhibited glomerulomegaly—a phenotype associated with early obesity and diabetic nephropathy.32 Interesting sex differences were observed where female mice exposed to HFD+AD demonstrated additive phenotypes.

Despite its profound public health implications, the mediators of CKM remain poorly defined, partly due to a dearth of animal models demonstrating all the features of metabolic, cardiovascular, and renal disorders. Cheng et al. used a high glucose and fructose model in mice to induce tubular damage and fibrosis.10 Along with HFD, Kim et al. used folic acid to injure renal tubules and fibrosis.9 This work did not characterize metabolic or cardiovascular phenotypes because their primary focus was renal progression and its protection from intervention. In addition, the translational relevance of some of these CKD models, such as folic acid, remains uncertain. By contrast, our work used the adenine-induced CKD model.12–15 Because tubular atrophy and interstitial fibrosis are the final common pathway for CKD, irrespective of inciting etiology, the adenine model represents advanced stage CKD (stage 4 and higher).16,17 Our model corresponds to CKM stage 3–4 with overt renal and metabolic along with PAD phenotypes.

The combination of HFD+AD demonstrated additive phenotypes in a few parameters and in a sex-specific manner. Male mice exposed to HFD+AD demonstrated a significant increase in immune cell (CD45+) infiltration in the ischemic hind limb alongside marked suppression of postexercise hyperemia (delta flux). By contrast, female mice in the HFD+AD group exhibited additive phenotypes across various functional measures—including distance traveled, time to exhaustion, and grip strength—despite the absence of a corresponding additive effect in limb perfusion. Delta flux examines the vascular response to exercise, while endurance testing (exhaustion, distance traveled) assesses vascular and muscle function. This pattern in females CKM mice suggests skeletal muscle dysfunction, in addition to microvascular defect, contributing to compromised endurance.

The lack of additive effects in other phenotypes may be explained as follows. First, adenine-induced CKD is an aggressive model of CKD resulting in a rapid GFR decline over 2 weeks and may limit the synergistic impact of HFD. A less aggressive model resulting in the GFR loss over weeks to months may uncover other CKM features. Second, the additive effect may be a function of time. Our study lasted for 6 weeks, which is typical for PAD manifestation. However, diabetes and metabolic syndrome-induced spontaneous myocardial fibrosis develop after 8–12 weeks and advanced fibrosis takes 4–6 months.33,34 Therefore, a longer duration of exposure may be required to uncover the additive effect of CKD and HFD. Third, the observed sex-based differences raise the possibility of hormonal influences as sex-specific mechanisms in CKM progression.

Importantly, this CKM model recapitulates the heterogeneity in human CKM phenotypes. As noted in the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey, patients with CKD and metabolic dysfunction did not consistently exhibit all cardiovascular and metabolic impairments.35 Instead, distinct subgroups were noted, marked by diverse trajectories and phenotypic combinations—ranging from clusters dominated by high heart rate, cholesterol, or BP to more paradoxical profiles with low diastolic BP and reduced eGFR. In addition, the ischemic hind limb dysfunction reflecting severe PAD in female mice is consistent with human studies. The US census data showed that the burden of PAD is higher in women for adults older than 40 years.36 In women, the risks for morbidity, mortality, and complications after intervention are increased with PAD.37,38 Overall, this CKM model can be explored to understand the sex-specific mechanisms underlying PAD pathogenesis in the context of CKM.

Importantly, this model can be used to investigate underlying molecular and cellular mechanisms in CKM, such as inflammatory pathways (e.g., TNF-α, IL-6), oxidative stress (e.g., mitochondrial dysfunction), endothelial dysfunction (e.g., reduced nitric oxide bioavailability, increased endothelin-1), fibrosis (e.g., TGF-β/suppressor of mothers against decapentaplegic protein signaling), and uremic toxicity-related signaling such as the Aryl hydrocarbon receptor axis. In addition, the model provides an opportunity to evaluate emerging combination therapies such as sodium-glucose contransporter 2 inhibitors or glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists.

Our study has limitations. The adenine-induced CKD model is a highly aggressive model. Future studies with a less aggressive CKD model should be explored with dietary and genetic models of metabolic disease to reveal more nuanced interactions between renal dysfunction and metabolic alterations. Other CKD rodent models representing different types of CKD can be combined with metabolic models. Detailed studies are needed to examine the sex-based differences in PAD and other cardiovascular phenotypes.
This animal model will pave the pathway to deepen the mechanistic understanding of cross-organ contribution to the pathogenesis of CKM and examine novel biomarkers and therapeutic targets.

ack.title: Acknowledgments
ack.p: The authors thank Mike Kirber at the Boston University Medical Center Imaging Core Facility for his assistance in imaging.

fn.p: S.L. and H.P. shares equal first authorship.
See related editorial, “A Novel Murine Model Integrating Peripheral Arterial Disease and Multiorgan Dysfunction: Unraveling Cardiovascular-Kidney-Metabolic Complexity,” on pages 221–222.

sec.title: Disclosures
sec.p: Disclosure forms, as provided by each author, are available with the online version of the article at http://links.lww.com/KN9/B334.

sec.title: Declarative Statements
sec.p: All animal experiments were conducted in accordance with the NIH Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals or an equivalent standard that meets or exceeds the ethical and welfare requirements outlined in the NIH Guide. All protocols were approved by the appropriate institutional animal care and use committee.

sec.title: Data Availability Statements
sec.p: Original data generated for the study will be made available upon reasonable request to the corresponding author. Data Type: Image Data, Research Protocols, and Statistical Analysis Plan.

sec.title: Supplemental Material
sec.p: This article contains the following supplemental material online at http://links.lww.com/KN9/B335.

Supplemental Figure 3. Muscle morphology and perfusion in female mice in ischemic and non-ischemic hind limb in different groups.

Supplemental Table 2. Antibodies used in this work.
